The typical formulation includes prebiotic fibers, selected botanical extracts (often things like berberine analogs, bitter melon compounds, or chromium forms), and sometimes adaptogenic herbs aimed at cortisol rhythm without heavy sedation. It fits least well for those who expect rapid weight loss, who have very sensitive digestion (high-FODMAP intolerance), or who rely heavily on medications that interact with botanicals (more on that later). One short aside: I've tested dozens of similar products over the years. The ones that stick around in my rotation are rarely the flashiest; they're the ones that quietly make mornings feel less urgent and evenings less snack-driven. Users who stick with bruno salomone for at least four weeks often report: These effects tend to build gradually. In my own three-week trial with a comparable formula last year, I tracked fasting windows extending from 14 to 16–17 hours on most days with less mental pushback. Bowel transit felt more predictable, which helped overall routine adherence. First, taste and texture matter for compliance. Gummies in this category often use pectin bases that can feel sticky or overly sweet; capsules avoid that but add swallowing friction for some. During one mini trial, I compared a gummy version to capsules—gummies won on convenience but lost on aftertaste (lingering herbal bitterness). Second, effects plateau. After 8–10 weeks, many report diminishing returns unless diet and sleep are dialed in tightly. It's supportive, not standalone. Third, cost adds up. At roughly $50–70 for 30 days, it's premium pricing. If you're already spending on quality protein, olive oil, and gym access, this might feel like an optional layer rather than essential. And here's a counterexample: A colleague tried a similar gut-metabolism gummy stack while traveling heavily for work. Despite consistent dosing, jet lag, irregular meals, and poor sleep negated any potential benefit. He felt no difference in energy or cravings—and actually gained a couple pounds from stress eating. The takeaway? Supplements amplify good habits; they don't create them. Evidence for individual ingredients in products like bruno salomone comes from peer-reviewed journals such as Diabetes Care, Nutrients, and Journal of Functional Foods, plus reviews from institutions like the Mayo Clinic and NIH Office of Dietary Supplements. Berberine-type compounds show moderate support for fasting glucose and insulin sensitivity in short-term trials (typically 8–12 weeks, 500–1500 mg/day). Chromium picolinate has mixed data for carb metabolism, with better signals in chromium-deficient populations. Prebiotic fibers (inulin, resistant starch) consistently improve microbiome metrics and satiety in meta-analyses published in Gut Microbes. However, high-quality evidence specifically on full bruno salomone-style blends is limited. Most studies are small (n<100), short-duration, and use isolated ingredients rather than proprietary combinations. Funding bias exists in some industry-sponsored trials, and long-term safety data (>1 year) is sparse for multi-botanical formulas. Plainly: individual components have plausible mechanisms, but the exact product synergy lacks large, independent RCTs. Effects vary widely by baseline diet, genetics, and microbiome starting point. In one practical breakdown I did, a leading competitor listed "proprietary gut blend 800 mg"—unhelpful. Bruno Salomone variants I've seen tend to disclose doses more openly, which builds trust. This table highlights tradeoffs in dosing, format, and verification. Bruno Salomone often sits in the middle-to-upper tier for transparency and balanced formulation. Red flags: flash-sale urgency, celebrity endorsements without disclosure, no contact info or batch numbers. One frequent mistake: starting too many changes at once. A client began bruno salomone, intermittent fasting, and a new gym program simultaneously. After two weeks of GI upset and fatigue, he blamed the supplement and quit. In reality, the fiber increase clashed with sudden carb restriction. Lesson: layer changes slowly—add the supplement first, stabilize for 2–3 weeks, then adjust diet. Another: ignoring timing. Taking it right before bed can disrupt sleep for sensitive people due to mild thermogenic effects. Morning or pre-meal dosing usually works better. And dosing friction—splitting gummies or forgetting capsules kills consistency. Set phone reminders or pair with breakfast. Is Bruno Salomone safe for long-term daily use?
Most users tolerate it well for 3–6 months, but cycle off every 12 weeks if you notice diminishing returns or mild GI shifts. Long-term human data on full blends remains limited. Can it replace a balanced diet?
No. It supports metabolic flexibility when diet is already solid. Relying on it alone rarely moves the needle. Will it help with keto flu or fasting adaptation?
Some report easier adaptation due to steadier energy and electrolyte-friendly botanicals, but evidence is anecdotal. Electrolytes and hydration matter more. Are there interactions with medications?
Possible with blood sugar-lowering drugs, thyroid meds, or anticoagulants. Check with a doctor if you're on prescriptions. How soon do people notice effects?
Satiety and energy smoothing often appear in 10–21 days; gut comfort can take 3–4 weeks. Patience is key. If you're curious, frame it as a short, structured trial. Take the recommended dose for 14 days while keeping diet, sleep, and activity logs. Track subjective markers: hunger timing, afternoon energy (1–10 scale), bowel patterns, and any side effects. Stop conditions: persistent GI distress beyond week 1, no noticeable satiety shift by day 12, or sleep disruption. If you feel neutral or slightly better and tolerate it, extend to 4–6 weeks for clearer patterns. This approach keeps expectations realistic and minimizes sunk cost. Lucas Bennett – The Practical Performance Optimizer
I specialize in testing supplements designed to support keto adherence and metabolic performance. Over the past five years, I’ve personally reviewed more than 80 consumer products, analyzing how they affect appetite control, daily consistency, digestive comfort, and long-term usability. My background in quality assurance and ingredient sourcing helps me evaluate formulation standards beyond surface-level claims. I focus on practical results — whether a supplement truly supports sustainable habits. This information is educational in nature and should not be interpreted as medical advice. title: The ADV That Combines Rugged Capability With Touring-Level Comfort - Top Speed
content: Adventure motorcycles sit at the top of the freedom mindset that a lot of riders have. They combine the ability to travel long distances for hours and hours in a single day, and yet, when the next day comes calling, they are all set to go out again. Over the course of a single day, these bikes can traverse mountains, go on endless highways, and, every once in a while, hit a patch of road so bad that it barely qualifies as one. But ADVs today aren’t just about getting out and getting there. They come with a level of sophistication usually reserved for four-wheelers. Of course, you won’t get the protection four doors offer you, but you will get very similar technology and a nice cushy seat, too. Most ADVs are capable of this, but there is one that stands out, whether it comes to touring comfort or its ability to kick things up in the dirt. Adventure bikes occupy a fascinating middle ground in the world of motorcycling. They’re not the fastest bikes on the track, and they’re not the lightest of off-roaders. Additionally, they’re not even as luxurious as a full-dress tourer. But they hit a kind of balance right at the center. These machines are built for versatility. Riders expect upright ergonomics, long-travel suspension, and engines that deliver usable power in a wide range of conditions. They’re meant to feel equally at home on a winding mountain road as they are navigating unpaved trails. On paved surfaces, the ADV is expected to behave like a refined touring machine. It should offer stability at high speeds, smooth power delivery, and sufficient wind protection, so the rider doesn’t feel blown back constantly. Because ADV riders spend hours in the saddle, the bike also has to offer a level of comfort that is not easy to get on conventional single-minded motorcycles. The saddle should be cushy, the riding position should be upright, the suspension needs to be tuned right, and a host of other things. Off the tarmac, the comfort factor goes away. Here, the ADV needs to be capable enough to climb over high rocks and go through deep ruts. Suspension tuning makes an appearance here again, as well as off-road worthy engine performance, high ground clearance, and tall handlebars for riders who stand on the footpegs. Weight distribution is another key factor here that allows a rider to balance his bike well in loose dirt sections. This Honda adventure bike offers the best balance of performance, capability, and smart pricing. Yes, you're going to find a variety even within this segment, but it's not really as vast as you think. Some bikes like the Triumph Tiger 1200 Explorer and the Kawasaki Versys 1100 are tuned for road use, offering suspension dynamics and wheels and tires made for asphalt. On the other hand, you have the Yamaha Tenere 700 and the Honda Africa Twin Adventure Sports that are biased towards the dirt. They have thinner seats, taller suspension, and larger wheels and tires for better traction. All of these bikes are capable wherever you push them, sure, and they all come with comfort levels that far surpass any other bike that’s not a tourer. But there is one standout bike that manages to blend everything together. It is amazingly comfortable when out and about on the streets and highways, but it has a level of capability in the dirt that few others seem to possess. The most affordable 300+ mile adventure touring bike on this list is priced under $7,000 and is powered by a single-cylinder engine. The BMW R 1300 GS Adventure steps into this world with a clear purpose: to be able to do everything the rider asks of it. And for that, we begin with the engine. At its core sits a 1,300cc boxer twin capable of producing 145 horsepower and 110 pound-feet of torque. The horizontally opposed cylinder layout has been a defining characteristic of the brand, but this new iteration is more compact and lighter than before. Power delivery is linear, and there is as much top-end speed for the highways as there is low-end grunt for tight technical trails. The gearbox has also been redesigned, and the weight distribution has been optimized for a more responsive and controlled ride regardless of the environment. BMW has introduced a completely new chassis with the R 1300 GS. The sheet-metal main frame and aluminum sub-frame centralize the mass and improve rigidity. There’s also the signature Telelever front and Paralever rear suspension setup, which reduces dive under braking, especially when the terrain gets rough. The addition of BMW’s latest Dynamic Suspension Adjustment allows the motorcycle to adapt damping and spring rates automatically, and while on the street, this makes the bike smoother; on the dirt, this can be invaluable. All of this contributes to a bike that feels planted on the streets even at higher speeds while still being able to maintain control on loose or rocky terrain. The GS gets a comprehensive suite of electronic aids to cater to the different, sometimes unpredictable, situations the rider is going to put the bike through. There are multiple riding modes that adjust throttle response, ABS, traction control, and suspension settings to suit different terrains. The Dynamic Traction Control and Engine Drag Torque Control work together to maintain the bike’s stability in low-grip situations, and cornering ABS ensures you don’t slide out under heavy braking in a corner. But one of the standout features is the optional radar-based system, which brings in adaptive cruise control and forward collision warning, adding a layer of security usually found in cars. The most fuel-efficient adventure motorcycle on this list is a city-friendly bike with an automatic gearbox and an under-$9,500 price tag. Comfort on the R 1300 GS isn’t an afterthought. BMW has a long history with adventure bikes ever since the R 80 G/S the company has only refined the segment. On the new R 1300 GS, the seating position is upright and neutral with a cushy, yet supportive seat designed for reduced strain on the back. Handlebars are positioned well to strike a balance between comfy highway touring and trail winding. Wind protection is refined as well with the adjustable windscreen. Heated grips, heated seats, and cruise control all make the journey that much less tiring for the rider. Now, despite this ADV's performance capabilities, it doesn’t shy away from being fairly mindful of its drinking habits, too. According to BMW, the bike is capable of returning a fuel efficiency figure of 48 MPG. Combine that with the Adventure’s 7.9-gallon fuel tank, and you’ll be traveling at least 379 miles before needing to stop for fuel. Maintenance and service intervals have also been considered, allowing you to stretch your riding days further. These adventure bikes are more at home away from home The modern ADV is not defined by compromise; it never was. While some trade a few characteristics in favor of others, they all perform manageably well in any situation. But with the BMW R 1300 GS Adventure, what you get is not one of those bikes that favor the road for the dirt or vice versa. It can and will do either. What it does tend to do better than most, however, is give the rider comfort and joy, the levels of which you will see on a small touring machine with a bigger engine. We want to hear from you! Share your opinions in the thread below and remember to keep it respectful.

title: [VIDEO] Breaking Down the Options on the Grand Sport from the Dealer’s GS Playbook - CorvetteBlogger

When Chevrolet comes out with a new Corvette, we’ve seen that they prepare a special “dealers brochure” called a playbook designed specifically for dealer sales reps. It’s a very informative video for those looking at what is possible when deciding on options for your Grand Sport build, and it serves as a nice introduction prior to getting the full Corvette Grand Sport’s official Order Guide as well. title: GS Caltex Sweeps Hyundai Engineering & Construction, Reaches Championship Finals - 조선일보
content: Gisele Silva's 32 points propel GS Caltex to finals as Yang Hyo-jin retires after Hyundai loss GS Caltex, led by ‘Cuba Express’ Gisele Silva, advanced to the women’s V-League championship finals after securing two consecutive victories over Hyundai Engineering & Construction. GS Caltex swept Hyundai Engineering & Construction 3-0 (25-23 25-23 25-19) in the second leg of the 2025-2026 V-League women’s playoff series held at Jangchung Gymnasium in Seoul on the 28th. Finishing third in the regular league, GS Caltex first defeated fourth-place Heungkuk Life in a single-match semifinal, then swept second-place Hyundai Engineering & Construction in the best-of-three playoff series with three straight wins. GS Caltex will face regular league champion Korea Expressway Corporation in the championship finals starting on the 1st of next month. The ‘main gun’ Silva’s firepower shone once again. Silva led the victory with 32 points, including four service aces. Kwon Min-ji (13 points) and Yoo Seo-yeon (8 points) also contributed from the backcourt. Hyundai Engineering & Construction’s combined efforts of foreign players Kari (12 points) and Justis (10 points) fell short of matching Silva’s individual tally. The team’s collapse made this match the final game for Hyundai Engineering & Construction’s franchise star Yang Hyo-jin, who announced her retirement at the end of this season. Yang Hyo-jin fought hard with 13 points but could not prevent the team’s defeat.

title: PRIMEIRO DIA No UBER BLACK Com BYD DOLPHIN GS✅ Veja Quanto Sobrou🤔 [4bbb81] - Fathom Journal
content:
Join over 9000 global opinion formers by subscribing to Fathom's influential Weekly Newsletter amy madigan showed up in my recommendations algorithm like every other trending thing I never asked for. Instagram, YouTube, Reddit—all suddenly decided I needed to know about this mysterious compound everyone's apparently been talking about. My first reaction? Skepticism so thick you could cut it with a scalpel. On my grad student budget, I can't afford to fall for marketing hype, but I'm also not the type to dismiss something without at least looking at the data. So I did what any good psychology PhD candidate would do: I went full research mode. The timing was actually perfect. I was in the middle of writing my lit review on cognitive enhancement pathways, so this became an unexpected side project. My advisor would kill me if she knew I was testing nootropics for "research purposes" during work hours, but technically I'm just surveying the literature, right? That's what I told myself anyway. The first thing I noticed is that amy madigan isn't a single compound—it's more of a category descriptor, which explains why everyone seems to have a different understanding of what we're even talking about. Some people mean a specific stack, others mean the general concept of certain cognitive support compounds. This ambiguity alone tells you something about how fragmented this space really is. The research I found suggests there's actually a decent body of preclinical work, but human data is scattered across dozens of small studies with inconsistent methodologies. Here's what gets me: the marketing has gotten so ahead of the evidence that it's almost impossible to have a rational conversation about it. Everyone's either evangelizing or dismissing, with very few people actually doing the work to separate signal from noise. That's exactly the kind of problem I find irresistible as a researcher. The term amy madigan gets thrown around in so many contexts that I spent the first week just trying to figure out what we were actually discussing. Was it a specific product? A class of compounds? A brand? The answer, frustratingly, is all of the above—and that's part of the problem. What I discovered is that amy madigan generally refers to a category of compounds that people use for cognitive support, particularly around focus, memory, and mental clarity. But here's where it gets messy: the specific formulations vary wildly between brands, the dosing protocols range from "conservative" to "aggressive," and the claimed mechanisms touch on everything from neurotransmitter modulation to neuroplasticity enhancement. That's a lot of ground to cover. I started digging through the academic literature on PubMed, focusing on systematic reviews and meta-analyses rather than individual studies. The research I found suggests there's genuine biological plausibility for several mechanisms these compounds might involve, but the translation from preclinical models to meaningful human effects is consistently messier than marketing materials would have you believe. What surprised me most was the publication bias situation. Positive studies get cited constantly in commercial contexts, while null findings or replication failures barely register in mainstream discourse. I found at least three recent meta-analyses that raised serious questions about effect sizes being inflated by small, poorly designed trials. This isn't unique to amy madigan—it's a pervasive problem in the supplement space—but it's worth noting because it directly affects how you should interpret the claims you'll encounter. The other thing that became clear quickly is that amy madigan occupies this weird regulatory gray zone where it can't make actual health claims but absolutely implies them through every marketing channel. The cognitive support angle is particularly fraught because everyone wants to believe there's a legal shortcut to better brain performance, and companies are more than happy to exploit that. I decided to run a structured self-experiment because, honestly, I wasn't going to trust the hype OR the dismissals without seeing how it felt in practice. For the price of one premium bottle—which would have cost me about $80—I could buy a month's supply of the more budget-friendly options and still have money left over for coffee. On my grad student budget, that's a meaningful calculation. The setup was simple: I chose three different amy madigan options representing different price points and formulation approaches. I tested each for approximately one week, keeping a daily log of sleep quality, subjective focus ratings, mood, and productivity markers. Was this scientifically rigorous? Absolutely not. But it's also exactly the kind of real-world testing that actually matters when you're evaluating whether something works for your specific situation. Week one was with a budget option that used a fairly standard stack. The effects, if I'm being honest, were subtle to the point of ambiguity. I noticed maybe a slight improvement in morning focus, but honestly, a proper breakfast and my first cup of coffee probably accounted for more variance than the supplement did. Week two involved a mid-range option with some different ratios and an additional compound I hadn't seen in the first one. This one was more noticeable—particularly in the afternoon crash period where I typically struggle to maintain momentum. The research I found suggests this might relate to how certain compounds affect adenosine processing, but I'm not willing to make that leap based on one person's experience. Week three brought a completely different approach: a higher-dose regimen focused on a single compound rather than a stacked approach. This was probably the most interesting from a mechanistic perspective because the effects were more distinct, though whether that's "better" depends entirely on what you're looking for. I felt more "wired" but also more prone to anxiety, which is a trade-off worth considering seriously. Throughout all three weeks, I maintained my baseline variables as much as possible: same sleep schedule, same workload, same caffeine intake. The diary entries got progressively more detailed as I tried to parse what was actually happening versus what I might be imagining. This is where I think most people go wrong—they expect dramatic, obvious effects when the reality is usually much more nuanced. Let me be direct about what I found: the evidence for amy madigan is somewhere between "promising but inconsistent" and "not yet convincing" depending on which outcomes you're measuring and how rigorously you evaluate the studies. Here's the breakdown that matters: The thing that bugs me most about this space is how impossible it is to do an apples-to-apples comparison. Everyone's stack is different, everyone's "standard dose" means something different, and everyone's measuring different outcomes. Some people track reaction time, others track subjective focus, and very few are tracking the things that actually matter long-term like sleep architecture or mood stability. What I can say from my own testing is that I noticed meaningful differences between formulations, but those differences didn't always align with price or marketing claims. The budget option actually performed comparably to the premium one for my specific use case, which raises questions about whether the additional cost is ever justified. One thing the research I found suggests repeatedly is that context matters enormously. Your baseline cognitive state, your sleep quality, your stress levels, and your individual biochemistry all modulate how these compounds might affect you. The guy writing the glowing review might have completely different biology than you do, which makes anecdotal evidence essentially useless as a decision-making tool. After all this research and experimentation, where do I actually land? Here's the honest answer: amy madigan is neither the miracle solution its evangelists claim nor the pointless scam its critics insist. It's a category of compounds with genuine biological activity, inconsistent quality control across the industry, and effects that vary significantly from person to person. Would I recommend it? That depends entirely on who you are and what you're hoping to achieve. If you're a student pulling late nights and looking for any edge, probably worth experimenting with the cheaper options first—if anything goes wrong, you haven't wasted much money, and if it works, you've found a useful tool. If you're expecting profound cognitive transformation, prepare for disappointment because that's not what the evidence supports. The research I found suggests that the people who benefit most are those with specific, addressable deficits—poor sleep quality, suboptimal nutrition, or particularly demanding cognitive periods. For someone already optimizing their basics, the marginal gains are going to be smaller and harder to notice. This matches what I experienced: the effects were most noticeable during my worst-performing periods, suggesting there's a ceiling on how much these compounds can help when your foundation is already solid. What I'd actually recommend is simpler than buying anything: fix your sleep, manage your stress, eat reasonably well, and get regular exercise. Those interventions have vastly more robust evidence than any supplement. But if you're already doing all that and still feel like you're falling short, then yes, amy madigan options are worth exploring—just approach it with realistic expectations and start cheap. Let me be more specific about who actually stands to benefit from this category, because I think a lot of people are wasting their money when they'd be better served by addressing fundamentals first. The people who might reasonably try amy madigan include graduate students in demanding programs (guilty as charged), professionals in high-cognitive-load roles, anyone dealing with temporary periods of unusual mental demand, and people who've already optimized the basics but still feel they're not performing at their desired level. The research I found suggests these populations show the most consistent positive responses in studies. Skip it entirely if you're not sleeping enough—these compounds cannot substitute for recovery, and you're likely just accelerating burnout. Skip it if you're looking for mood enhancement without addressing underlying issues, because some formulations can worsen anxiety. Skip it if you're on any medications without talking to someone who understands interactions, because this space is poorly regulated and contamination or unexpected interactions are real risks. The other thing worth mentioning is that this is a space where your mileage will absolutely vary. What worked for me might not work for you, and vice versa. The budget-first approach I mentioned earlier isn't just about cost—it's about finding what actually works for your specific system without blowing money on marketing-loaded premium products that might not deliver proportional benefits. For the price of one premium bottle, I could buy several months of budget options and run proper experiments to find what works. That's the approach I'd suggest for anyone genuinely curious. Just remember: these are tools, not solutions, and they work best when you're already doing the foundational work that makes them meaningful. Country: United States, Australia, United Kingdom. title: The Motorcycle That Can Handle Just About Everything - Top Speed
content: We wish all motorcycles could be do-it-all creations, but these days, even a single source of income isn’t enough to handle every expense in life. So it would be unfair to expect something as niche a product as a motorcycle to be the answer to every question when it comes to riding. That’s what makes them so much fun, too. The fact that they’re specialized means that they are perfect at whatever it is they have been developed for. Yes, sometimes that can be a little demanding on the rider, because if you’re going to ride a fast motorcycle, you’ll need to be fit, so time dedicated to physical fitness is a prerequisite. But we’ll keep circling back to that same wish, that of a motorcycle that could do it all. Wouldn’t that be great? There are many different kinds of motorcycles available today, with niches within niches that appeal to all kinds of prospective customers. There are a few kinds that will do a lot of different things. There are cruisers that can double up as tourers, for example. All they need is wind protection and a way to mount luggage. Sport tourers are another type of motorcycle that are capable of a great many different kinds of things, especially the crossover-type ones. They love corners, are long-legged enough for multiple-day rides, can carry a pillion in comfort, and, when a crossover, can also handle a little bit of off-tarmac work. Dual-sport bikes would be a counterpoint to sport tourers, with a preference for dirt and an ability to cover ground on the highway. There is a shortage of large-capacity modern dual-sport bikes today that can handle the highway, although the few that are capable of it are genuinely good products. That leaves us with adventure bikes, and that seems to be the best bet if we’re looking for a motorcycle that can handle just about everything. ADVs can tour, are at ease with dirt, love corners, and in some cases, can deal with the commute as well with equanimity. BMW’s GS series of motorcycles is highly respected for many reasons, including reliability, fun factor, and sheer ability. The R 1300 GS is the latest flagship, and it upholds the reputation of what is widely acknowledged to be the leader in its segment. BMW hasn’t reinvented the wheel here; the 1300 GS follows the same playbook as the 1250 GS, which does the same with the 1200 GS. There are improvements, of course – it is lighter, more powerful, and has more electronics and capability than ever before. With the different accessories available, it can be customized to whatever conditions you’re going to ride around in the most. That means it is the best candidate for the title of the motorcycle that can handle just about everything. The R 1300 GS is undoubtedly a premium product, but its base price of $20,395 is unexpectedly competitive. You get a hugely capable product that can adapt to any situation, and with one of the most powerful engines in the segment. Adding accessories can raise the price in a hurry to a very premium one, especially since you have no option but to opt for one of the add-on ‘packages’ that BMW is known for. Still, that doesn't mean that the base R 1300 GS isn’t capable of handling just about everything. The most affordable adventure motorcycle here is a sub-$6,000 Royal Enfield with a 40-horsepower mill, a TFT screen, and switchable traction and ABS. The 1,300 cc boxer twin is an evolutionary design. There realistically wasn’t anything to add – the R 1200 GS got liquid cooling, and the R 1250 GS got variable valve timing, so all there was were refinements. The compression ratio of the R 1300 GS engine is 13.3:1, and it generates 145 horsepower at 7,750 RPM and 110 pound-feet at 6,500 RPM. These are quite high figures for the segment, irrespective of the number of cylinders. What is nice about it is that it doesn’t feel intimidating. It is tuned so that the learning curve isn’t steep, and as long as you’ve ridden big ADVs before, it is very easy to handle. The electronics are there, but they work seamlessly in the background, although setting it up might feel a little intimidating for a first timer, with the amount of options there are. Special mention needs to be made of the optional automatic gearbox: this is an automated manual that does all the shifting for you, and you can switch off the feature if you want to change gears yourself. It is an option that needs one of the add-on packs and drives the price up significantly, but if you truly want to do everything with a single motorcycle, this option makes a lot of sense. The largest GS has always had a unique setup that no other product in its segment uses. It has a frame that is more often seen on road bikes, a diamond frame with the engine as a stressed member. This helps it keep weight down, and makes its on-road manners predictable. There is a double-wishbone front suspension coupled with a linkage-type shock at the rear with a single-sided swingarm. Suspension travel is 7.5 inches at the front and 7.8 inches at the rear. It might feel a little different from a conventional telescopic front setup, but it sure is capable. If you are shopping for an ADV on a budget, it is well worth considering one of these used bikes The brakes aren’t radical, with conventional dual 310 mm discs and radial four-piston fixed calipers at the front, and a 285 mm disc with a two-piston floating caliper at the rear. The wheels are a 19/17 inch combination, and the base model has an alloy wheel set with tubeless tires. You can upgrade the brakes to better front units, you can upgrade to electronic suspension, and you can replace the stock alloy wheels with forged enduro rims or cross-spoke laced rims. The R 1300 GS starts off with a 6.5-inch TFT screen, which is one of the largest in the segment. You can have the R 1300 GS Adventure if you plan on doing long-distance touring, because its 7.9-gallon fuel tank gives it one of the highest tank ranges in all of the world. With the standard R 1300 GS, though, you can have a lot of customization with the different ‘packages’ that BMW offers. There are individual items like a center stand, an electrically adjustable windscreen, engine protection, adjustable footpegs, and adjustable foot levers that are available, but they invariably require you to purchase a package first. Elsewhere, the electronics are just as advanced as anything in the segment, especially when you add options like the electronic suspension and radar-based active cruise control. It has a by-wire throttle and six-axis IMU by default, so you get ride modes, adjustable traction control, switchable ABS, wheel lift mitigation, and cornering functionality wherever available. The electronic suspension auto-lowers at walking speeds like other bikes in the segment, but it also self-levels, which is nice. The list includes everything from small and bash-friendly ADVs to proper globe-trotter heavyweights There are very few options if you truly want a motorcycle that is both indestructible and capable of being truly enjoyable in all conditions. If you’re on a budget, the Honda Africa Twin ($15,199) will do nicely. It might not have a very powerful engine, but it definitely has the pace and reliability, not to mention the ability, to handle it all. With the Adventure Sports, you get a 6.6-gallon fuel tank and electronic suspension, while the automatic gearbox is an option for either variant. Then there is the Triumph Tiger 1200 GT Explorer ($23,975), which has a lot of features identical to the BMW: a single-sided swingarm, shaft drive, and Showa semi-active suspension. It runs a unique inline three-cylinder engine with an odd firing order, but its weight is that of a twin-cylinder-powered example in the segment. So you get the best of both worlds. The most expensive option is also the one that comes closest to the BMW’s breadth of abilities. $31,995 might be a tall order, but the Ducati Multistrada V4 Rally has everything you’d possibly need or want to cross continents in comfort. The Multistrada’s Achilles Heel, the Desmodromic valvetrain, has gone, replaced by conventional valve springs in the Granturismo engine. It makes a full 170 horsepower reliably, with industry-leading service intervals and valve clearance checks, and has electronic suspension and a big 7.9-gallon fuel tank. We want to hear from you! Share your opinions in the thread below and remember to keep it respectful.

title: Goldman Sachs Canada Inc. Reports Acquisition and Disposition of Common Shares of Altus Group Limited - Yahoo Finance Singapore
content: Tip: Try a valid symbol or a specific company name for relevant results TORONTO, March 27, 2026 /CNW/ - Goldman Sachs Canada Inc. ("GS Canada") announces that, on March 18, 2026, it acquired an aggregate of 4,204 common shares of Altus Group Limited ("Altus") through the facilities of the Toronto Stock Exchange and/or on other Canadian marketplaces. Immediately following such acquisition, GS Canada had control or direction over an aggregate of 2,153,379 common shares of Altus representing approximately 5.32% of the 40,490,242 common shares of Altus (excluding 78,205 escrowed common shares) that GS Canada understands to be outstanding. GS Canada acquired a total of 12,804 common shares of Altus (inclusive of the 4,204 common shares of Altus referred to above) since the commencement of Altus' substantial issuer bid and has subsequently sold common shares of Altus such that GS Canada currently owns less than 5% of the 40,490,242 common shares of Altus referred to above. The common shares of Altus were acquired by GS Canada for hedging purposes in the ordinary course of GS Canada's business. The securities referred to above were acquired and are held in the ordinary course of business and were not acquired and are not held for the purpose of or with the effect of changing or influencing the control of the issuer of the securities and were not acquired and are not held in connection with or as a participant in any transaction having that purpose or effect. GS Canada may, in the future and from time to time, acquire or dispose of common shares of Altus in the ordinary course of business. This press release is issued and filed in accordance with section 5.4 of National Instrument 62-104 – Take-Over Bids and Issuer Bids.
